title: The Amazon and PayPal of Latin America

transcript.text: I'm down here in the capital city of
Uruguay, Monte Vido, to discuss one of
the country's biggest success stories,
Marcato Libre, the Amazon, and PayPal of
Latin America. Unlike PayPal, they're
actually growing like wildfire in
multiple geographies. What's not to like
about Marcato Libre? That's exactly what
we're going to talk about today,
starting with looking at what an
investment in Marcato Libre entails.
Now, this isn't the first time that we
discussed Melly back in November of
2023. We looked at this ecommerce and
fintech giant, noting that they had an
exceptional amount of competition in the
geographies that they operate in, and we
said we were going to avoid the stock
going forward. So, it's always
interesting to see what happened since
we said that. And when you benchmark the
performance of this stock over the past
3.4 years, you see they actually
underperformed their benchmark NASDAQ.
So NASDAQ returned 115%, Melly returned
84% which actually beat the S&P 500
return of 72%. So we haven't missed much
and there's always this feeling of
comfort that years later you can see the
progress of the company and still get in
at presumably a reasonable price. Now,
share price performance doesn't tell you
anything about valuation, right? That's
what we want to look at to determine
whether we're getting a better deal
today now than we were before. And to do
that, we can use our simple valuation
ratio. That's market cap divided by
annualized revenues. It's a little bit
more responsive than traditional price
to sales. And when we calculate that
number across more than 150 disruptive
tech stocks in our tech stock catalog,
we get about six. Well, Mellie's trading
under three. So, one could argue that
it's meaningfully undervalued. And
what's interesting to note is this
decline over time. You always hear these
instant analysts on social media talking
about Marie rate when they just want the
stock they're holding to suddenly
magically appreciate. Right on Wall
Street, that term is sometimes used to
reflect market sentiment where a
valuation will adjust to sort of a new
mean. Well, Marie rate actually works on
the downside and one can't help but
wonder here why this stock is seeing a
depreciation in valuation. Well, one
reason could be this perception that all
the lowhanging fruit has now been
picked. We would fully expect to see
margin pressures as they fight to keep
up the spectacular growth that they've
been seeing. of particular interest
would be their biggest market, Brazil,
which makes up more than 50% of the
revenues. By the way, I'm on my way this
week to Brazil to work on our investing
in Brazil video, possibly a piece on New
Bank. So, make sure you're subscribed so
you don't miss those pieces and turn
notifications on. So, as it turns out,
our theory is right. We can look at the
latest investor deck from Melly and they
say it right here. The margin
compression that you see reflects our
decision to invest in key long-term
growth opportunities. So indeed margins
are being compressed and that's a
concern, right? Because you're investing
in cash generating machines and perhaps
this machine can't generate as much cash
as we'd like to see despite their
growth. Now some questions about Melly
we're going to want to take a look at
today. What geographies are we getting
exposure to here for fintech side of the
business and e-commerce side of the
business that can create a rather
complex competitive analysis that we'll
poke at a bit? Is this more Amazon
exposure or PayPal exposure? And what's
the profitability profile of these two
businesses for the Amazon exposure?
What's going on with the real Amazon?
That was a concern that we had 3 years
ago, right? That's a really difficult
player to compete against, right? Last
time we checked, Amazon was investing
heavily in Brazil. How are those
investments working for them? For PayPal
exposure, it simply comes down to this.
How much exposure are we getting here to
consumer debt? We don't like that. What
are key metrics investors ought to be
looking at? And could we ever stop
worrying and start liking Melly? Well,
first, when we break down revenues by
geography, we see three key geographies.
Brazil, Argentina, and Mexico, we see
spectacular growth across this business.
So, consolidated growth year-over-year
of 44% is just simply remarkable. Now,
what's interesting is to see that
Argentina is actually the lowest growth
geographical segment for their business.
Now, I haven't historically thought of
Argentina as a great place to invest in,
and we'll talk about that in a second.
But what's notable here is that you're
making a big bet on Brazil. So last
quarter, the exposure you had to Brazil
actually increased to 53% from about
52%. So that's something you want to
consider. And when we look at the
country contribution to fintech
revenues, we see that 28% comes from
Argentina. Well, what's interesting
about Argentina, I recall the last time
I was there, this could have been over a
decade ago, having to go exchange
dollars on the black market and getting
a 30% premium. So basically in countries
like that, you get 30% appreciation by
giving them US dollars on the black
market. You get 30% more local currency
than you would by the bank. When that
happens, a country is broken, right? And
apparently that no longer is the case in
Argentina. And country risk there is
dropped dramatically over time. To look
at country risk, which you all should,
everybody always looks at the upside.
Few people look at the downside. This
study by the Adam Smith Center for
Economic Freedom gives you sort of a
framework of risk. When you're examining
these sorts of places, they actually
rank the various countries we're looking
at today. Surprisingly, Mexico ranks the
highest in terms of risk, Brazil the
lowest, but Argentina actually seeing
their risk decline over time. Now,
before we move on to talking about
profitability for Melly, I wanted to
quickly send a message to all our paying
subscribers who made this video
possible. As I've said before, we're
never going to increase your
subscription prices. What you pay now
will be what you pay for as long as you
remain subscribed for life. We keep
improving the quality of the
subscription offering. You keep paying
the same price. We increase your
lifetime value. However, for everyone
else, after 5 years of keeping prices
the same, we're going to be increasing
our subscription prices for new
subscribers at the end of this month.
So, if you've ever considered
subscribing to Nanalyze Premium, now's
the time to do it and lock in the
current lower price for life. Info in
the pinned comment below. Now let's talk
profitability for Melly. So first thing
we can do chart gross margins and
operating margins over time. We see
gross margins actually taking quite a
dip there. So let's start with gross
margins on the bottom chart here. You
see this steady decline over time but
still hovering over 40%. I that's pretty
decent, right? But when we look at
operating margins, we sort of see from
the second half of 2023 onwards this
steady decline, right? and it's
affecting valuation. So that makes us
want to answer this question, how
profitable are these two segments,
right? E-commerce and fintech. Well,
what they do provide us with is this
number called direct contribution
margin. And for the accountants in the
audience, I've put the definition for
that. All this tells us really is that
fintech is a bit more profitable than
the e-commerce side, but not by very
much. So that's useful to know. So
speaking of the fintech segment, let's
try to drill into it a bit and
understand the consumer debt exposure.
So I looked at the academic research on
this and it does appear conclusive
across multiple sources that consumer
debt has been this asset class if you
will that's outperformed historically in
the short term but not the longer term.
Right? And that's intuitive. Why?
Because it's very easy to grow credit
revenue for any business, right? It's
very easy to lax your requirements and
and grow that very quickly. Indeed, we
see credit revenue growing very strong
last year, 77% compared to the other
component of their fintech revenues,
which is financial services and income,
which grew 33%. Well, when we go and dig
into that credit portfolio, what we see
here is that credit cards and consumer
debt make up over 80% of their debt
exposure. So, that isn't very appealing
to us at all. So, takeaways so far,
credit revenue is almost a majority of
fintech revenues. 80% of that is exposed
to consumers across these various
geographies. valuation of the company is
declining, reflecting their weakening
margins. Argentina is their slowest
growing market, but they're also
dominating in both fintech and
e-commerce in Argentina. So maybe
they're maxing out. We're going to take
a look at that in a second. Brazil is
most of the exposure you're getting, but
what sort of growth is left in that
market? More importantly, for their
biggest market, what does competition
look like? Now, competitive analysis, as
I said, is tough. And here's why. You
need to break down the competition
basically across six different segments.
E-commerce for their three major markets
and fintech for their three major
markets. And here on the left, I've
broken out the percentage of revenues
coming from each. So, for example,
Brazil and Mexico e-commerce accounts
for nearly half of the company's
revenue. So, it' be very important to
know who their competitor is in Brazil
and Mexico. Their biggest competitor of
course being Amazon. So you can see here
that in Mexico in particular, they're
head-to-head against Amazon. They're a
proper rival. So that's quite a
dangerous space for them right now where
they're probably having to sink a lot of
money into trying to beat Amazon.
Brazil, they're the leader, but Amazon
is still creeping up quite quickly. Then
when you go down to fintech, what is
interesting here is that for Brazil and
Mexico, fintech, so that accounts for
about a third of their revenues. New
bank is their key competitor in both
geographies. And Melly seems to be
really dominating on the fintech side in
Mexico against New Bank. In Brazil, it's
more of a nuanced conversation, right?
So you can see the complexity here in
doing a competitive analysis. Note that
for Argentina, they're absolutely
dominating in e-commerce and fintech. So
that's great to see. And because Brazil
is such an important market for them, I
pulled up this additional competitive
landscape. This is from the US
government and of course they misspelled
Macato Libre there. Not sure why, but
you can see Marcato Libre they say has
35% market share. Amazon at 20%. In
third place, Shopppee. This is the SCA
group out of Asia that people keep
bringing up wanting us to cover. Maybe
down the road we will. Premium
subscribers can raise that in our
inviteonly discord server. Now for the
fintech segment in Brazil, they say
Marcato leads in volume and ecosystem
integration whilst New Bank is the
undisputed leader in active user base
and profitability per user. So the bold
thesis for Melly, I think plenty of
people talk about that the opportunity
is so large. One might say that there's
room for multiple competitors across
geographies and segments. They dominate
in Argentina. That's an improving
economy. So, they can keep benefiting
there. The company is certainly not
overpriced based on revenuebased
valuation metrics that reflects
asymmetric bet. Right? Your downside is
potentially protected by that low
valuation. The growth that they're
seeing off such a large base is
incredibly strong. Make no mistake about
that. And when you think about fintech
and e-commerce, these two segments
complement each other very well.
Competitors, their key competitors, New
Bank and Amazon are doing one or the
other, right? So, they don't have that
advantage. And Melly can replicate this
success they're seeing in other
geographies. I'd be curious to see which
one is next. You see the name Chile
mentioned, but I'm not sure where or
when they're going to be able to show
one more geography as they've done with
their three major geographies. Right
now, when it comes to the bare thesis, I
think there's three points to make here.
Amazon has much deeper pockets. Melly,
on the other hand, has been increasing
their debt load because their operating
cash flows aren't sufficient enough for
them to combat Amazon. This is basically
48% of Melliey's revenues under attack
by the buff bald gorilla and his mate
with the giant lips. Number two, half
their fintech revenues relate to credit,
of which 80% are consumer debt. Not
attractive at all. And number three,
margin compression means they eventually
need to win here or it's going to turn
into a death spiral. So, what would it
take for us to move this company from an
avoid to a like? Well, first of all,
we'd probably want to better understand
their largest market, Brazil, a whole
lot better. So, we have a coming video,
as I said, on investing in Brazil. As
for their two major competitors in
Brazil and Mexico, Amazon and New Bank,
I think we're going to do a piece on
Amazon just independently because I
don't think we've ever covered that
firm. It would be interesting. And we'll
probably take another look at New Bank,
which we've looked at in the past in
numerous pieces. Now, we're going to
keep premium subscribers updated on our
invite only Discord server and of course
our Nanalyze Market open, a daily
trading email that paying customers get
every trading day. Now, I'm going to
leave you with this piece we did on an
emerging markets play we do like quite a
bit that also enjoys a very reasonable
valuation right now. Give that a watch
next. Thanks so much for taking the time
to watch this video today.
